title: System Lockout — Retries Exceeded
shortDescription: The furnace has attempted ignition multiple times and failed, entering safety lockout.
fullDescription: A single LED blink on a Carrier furnace indicates that the system has locked out after exceeding the maximum number of ignition retry attempts. The control board tried to ignite the burners several times without success, and has now shut down as a safety measure. The lockout will automatically reset after approximately one to three hours, or you can manually reset the system by cycling power.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: If you smell gas, leave the home immediately and call your gas company from outside. Do not operate any electrical switches. Always turn off the gas supply before attempting any repairs.

causes.cause: Faulty or dirty igniter (hot surface igniter or spark igniter)
Gas valve not opening properly
Flame sensor dirty or malfunctioning
Low gas pressure or gas supply issue
Cracked or damaged igniter element

symptoms: Furnace does not produce heat
Single LED blink on the control board
Furnace starts the ignition sequence but flames never appear
Repeated clicking sound from igniter with no flame
System shuts down after multiple ignition attempts

fixSteps.title: Power Cycle the Furnace
fixSteps.instruction: Turn the furnace power switch off, wait 30 seconds, and turn it back on to reset the lockout. Observe if ignition is successful on the next cycle.

fixSteps.title: Clean the Flame Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the flame sensor rod in the burner assembly. Gently clean it with fine steel wool or emery cloth to remove oxidation buildup. Reinstall and test.
tools: Fine steel wool or emery cloth

fixSteps.title: Inspect the Igniter
fixSteps.instruction: Visually check the hot surface igniter for cracks or damage. If it glows orange during ignition attempts, it may be weak. Measure resistance with a multimeter; a good igniter typically reads 40-200 ohms.

fixSteps.title: Verify Gas Supply
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure the gas shutoff valve is fully open. Check that other gas appliances in the home are working. If no other appliances work, contact your gas utility.

fixSteps.title: Check Gas Valve Operation
fixSteps.instruction: Listen for the gas valve clicking open during the ignition sequence. If the igniter glows but no gas flows, the gas valve may need replacement by a licensed technician.

whenToCallPro: If you smell gas at any time, if the igniter or gas valve needs replacement, or if the problem persists after cleaning the flame sensor and resetting.

faq.q: How long does a Carrier furnace lockout last?
faq.a: The automatic lockout resets after 1-3 hours depending on the model. You can also manually reset by turning the power off and back on.

faq.q: Can I run my furnace after a lockout?
faq.a: After a reset, the furnace will attempt ignition again. If it locks out repeatedly, there is an underlying issue that needs to be addressed.

faq.q: How much does a new furnace igniter cost?
faq.a: A replacement hot surface igniter typically costs $15-$40 for the part. Professional installation runs $100-$250.

title: Pressure Switch Stuck Open
shortDescription: The pressure switch failed to close, indicating a venting or draft inducer problem.
fullDescription: Two LED blinks on a Carrier furnace indicate that the pressure switch did not close or is stuck open. The pressure switch is a safety device that verifies the draft inducer motor is creating adequate airflow through the heat exchanger and venting system before gas is released. If the switch does not close, the furnace will not proceed with ignition.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: If you smell gas, leave the home immediately and call your gas company from outside. Do not operate any electrical switches. Never bypass or jump the pressure switch as this defeats a critical safety mechanism.

causes.cause: Blocked or restricted exhaust vent or intake pipe
Failed draft inducer motor
Cracked or disconnected pressure switch hose
Faulty pressure switch
Condensate drain clogged (in high-efficiency furnaces)

symptoms: Furnace does not ignite — draft inducer runs but burners never light
Two LED blinks on the control board
Draft inducer motor runs continuously without progressing to ignition
Humming sound from draft inducer but no heat produced

fixSteps.title: Inspect Exhaust and Intake Vents
fixSteps.instruction: Go outside and check the exhaust and intake PVC pipes for blockages such as ice, snow, bird nests, or debris. Clear any obstructions found.

fixSteps.title: Check the Pressure Switch Hose
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the small rubber hose connected to the pressure switch. Ensure it is firmly attached at both ends and has no cracks, holes, or water trapped inside. Remove and blow through it to clear any blockage.

fixSteps.title: Clear the Condensate Drain
fixSteps.instruction: For high-efficiency furnaces, check the condensate drain line and trap. Clear any clogs by flushing with warm water or using a small brush.

fixSteps.title: Test the Pressure Switch
fixSteps.instruction: Using a multimeter, check continuity across the pressure switch terminals while the inducer motor is running. The switch should show continuity (closed) when the inducer is on.

fixSteps.title: Inspect the Draft Inducer Motor
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the draft inducer motor is spinning at full speed and not making unusual noises. A weak or failing motor may not generate sufficient draft to close the pressure switch.

whenToCallPro: If the draft inducer motor is not working, if the pressure switch tests faulty, or if vents are clear but the problem persists.

faq.q: Can I bypass the pressure switch to get heat?
faq.a: Never bypass the pressure switch. It is a critical safety device that ensures combustion gases are being properly vented out of your home.

faq.q: Why does this happen in winter?
faq.a: In cold weather, ice and snow can block the exhaust or intake vents outside your home. Check them regularly during winter storms.

faq.q: How much does a pressure switch cost?
faq.a: A replacement pressure switch typically costs $20-$50 for the part. Professional installation runs $150-$300.

title: Pressure Switch Stuck Closed
shortDescription: The pressure switch is closed before the draft inducer starts, indicating a stuck switch or wiring issue.
fullDescription: Three LED blinks on a Carrier furnace indicate the pressure switch is in the closed position before the draft inducer motor has started. Under normal operation, the switch should be open when the inducer is off and close only after the inducer creates sufficient draft. A stuck-closed switch could allow the furnace to operate unsafely.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: If you smell gas, leave the home immediately and call your gas company from outside. A stuck-closed pressure switch is a serious safety concern as it could allow the furnace to fire without proper venting.

causes.cause: Pressure switch stuck in closed position mechanically
Short circuit in pressure switch wiring
Incorrect pressure switch installed (wrong rating)
Water in pressure switch hose creating false reading

symptoms: Furnace refuses to start any part of the ignition sequence
Three LED blinks on the control board
Draft inducer motor does not start
Error appears immediately when thermostat calls for heat

fixSteps.title: Disconnect and Inspect Pressure Switch Hose
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the rubber hose from the pressure switch and check for water trapped inside. Drain any water and reattach the hose securely.

fixSteps.title: Test the Pressure Switch
fixSteps.instruction: With the furnace off and inducer not running, use a multimeter to check for continuity across the pressure switch. It should show no continuity (open) when the inducer is off.

fixSteps.title: Check Wiring for Shorts
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the wires running to the pressure switch for any damage, bare spots, or places where wires may be touching each other or the chassis.

fixSteps.title: Replace the Pressure Switch
fixSteps.instruction: If the switch reads closed without the inducer running, it is faulty and must be replaced. Match the exact part number for your Carrier model.

whenToCallPro: If you are unsure about testing electrical components, or if replacing the switch does not resolve the issue.

faq.q: Is a stuck-closed pressure switch dangerous?
faq.a: Yes, it could potentially allow the furnace to fire without verifying proper venting. The control board detects this condition and prevents operation as a safety measure.

faq.q: Can water cause this error?
faq.a: Yes, condensation or water trapped in the pressure switch hose can create a false pressure reading that makes the switch appear stuck closed.

faq.q: What pressure switch do I need for my Carrier furnace?
faq.a: The pressure switch rating must match your specific model. Check the furnace data plate or the old switch for the part number. Using the wrong rating can cause operational or safety issues.

title: Open High Limit Switch
shortDescription: The high limit switch has tripped due to overheating, shutting down the furnace for safety.
fullDescription: Four LED blinks on a Carrier furnace indicate that the high limit switch (also called the upper limit or rollout limit) has opened due to excessive temperature in the furnace. This safety device shuts down the burners when the heat exchanger or furnace cabinet temperature exceeds safe levels, typically caused by restricted airflow.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: If you smell gas, leave the home immediately and call your gas company. A furnace that repeatedly overheats may have a cracked heat exchanger, which can leak carbon monoxide. Ensure you have working CO detectors in your home.

causes.cause: Dirty or clogged air filter restricting airflow
Blocked or closed supply/return vents in the home
Failed blower motor or capacitor
Dirty evaporator coil restricting airflow
Faulty high limit switch

symptoms: Furnace starts but shuts off after a few minutes
Four LED blinks on the control board
Warm air initially, then furnace stops producing heat
Furnace short-cycles repeatedly
Hot air or burning smell near the furnace

fixSteps.title: Replace the Air Filter
fixSteps.instruction: Check and replace the furnace air filter. A heavily clogged filter is the most common cause of overheating. Use the correct size filter for your system.

fixSteps.title: Check All Vents and Registers
fixSteps.instruction: Walk through the home and ensure all supply and return air vents are open and unobstructed by furniture, rugs, or curtains.

fixSteps.title: Inspect the Blower Motor
fixSteps.instruction: With the furnace running in fan-only mode, verify the blower motor is operating at full speed. Listen for unusual sounds indicating bearing failure.

fixSteps.title: Check the Blower Capacitor
fixSteps.instruction: If the blower motor hums but does not spin, the run capacitor may be faulty. Visually inspect for bulging or leaking. Test with a multimeter on the capacitance setting.

fixSteps.title: Reset the High Limit Switch
fixSteps.instruction: Some limit switches have a manual reset button. Press the reset button once and test the system. If it trips again, the underlying cause has not been resolved.

whenToCallPro: If changing the filter and opening vents does not resolve the issue, if the blower motor is not working, or if you suspect a cracked heat exchanger.

faq.q: How often should I change my furnace filter?
faq.a: Standard 1-inch filters should be changed every 1-3 months. Thicker media filters (4-5 inch) can last 6-12 months. Check monthly during heavy use.

faq.q: Can a dirty filter damage my furnace?
faq.a: Yes, restricted airflow from a dirty filter causes overheating, which stresses the heat exchanger and can lead to cracks over time. Regular filter changes are essential.

faq.q: Why does my furnace keep overheating?
faq.a: Recurring overheating usually points to an airflow problem: dirty filter, blocked vents, failing blower motor, or dirty evaporator coil. Less commonly, a faulty limit switch or cracked heat exchanger.

title: Flame Sensed Without Gas Valve Call
shortDescription: The control board detects flame when the gas valve should be closed, indicating a dangerous condition.
fullDescription: Five LED blinks on a Carrier furnace indicate that the flame sensor is detecting flame when the gas valve has not been commanded to open. This is a serious safety condition that could indicate a leaking gas valve or a faulty flame sensing circuit.
safetyWarning: CRITICAL GAS SAFETY WARNING: This code may indicate a gas valve leak. If you smell gas, leave your home immediately, do not operate any switches, and call 911 or your gas company from outside. Do not attempt to repair this issue yourself.

causes.cause: Gas valve leaking through (not fully closing)
Short circuit in flame sensor wiring
Faulty control board misreading flame sensor signal
Residual flame from previous cycle (delayed flame out)

symptoms: Furnace will not operate — locked out immediately
Five LED blinks on the control board
Possible gas odor near the furnace (evacuate immediately if detected)
Furnace shuts down before ignition sequence begins

fixSteps.title: Check for Gas Odor
fixSteps.instruction: Carefully sniff near the furnace for any gas odor. If you detect gas, leave the home immediately and call your gas company or 911.

fixSteps.title: Power Cycle the System
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the furnace at the power switch and the gas shutoff valve. Wait 5 minutes. Turn the gas back on, then restore power. If the code returns immediately, do not continue troubleshooting yourself.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Flame Sensor Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: With the furnace powered off, check the wiring from the flame sensor to the control board. Look for bare wire, damage, or places where the wire may be grounding against the chassis.

fixSteps.title: Call a Licensed Technician
fixSteps.instruction: This code typically requires professional diagnosis and repair. A technician will test the gas valve for leakage and verify the flame sensing circuit.

whenToCallPro: Immediately. This code indicates a potentially dangerous condition that should be diagnosed by a licensed HVAC technician.

faq.q: Is this error code dangerous?
faq.a: Yes, it can indicate a gas valve that is not closing properly, which could lead to a gas leak. Have it inspected by a professional as soon as possible.

faq.q: Can I fix this myself?
faq.a: This is not a DIY repair. A leaking gas valve or faulty flame sensing circuit requires professional diagnosis and repair for safety reasons.

faq.q: How much does a gas valve replacement cost?
faq.a: A gas valve replacement typically costs $200-$600 including parts and labor from a licensed technician.

title: Line Voltage Polarity Reversed or Ground Fault
shortDescription: The furnace has detected reversed hot and neutral wiring or a grounding issue.
fullDescription: Six LED blinks on a Carrier furnace indicate that the control board has detected a line voltage polarity issue. The hot and neutral wires may be reversed at the furnace connection, or there may be a grounding fault. Many modern furnace control boards require correct polarity to operate the flame sensing circuit properly.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: Electrical work can be dangerous. Turn off power at the breaker before checking any wiring. If you are not comfortable working with electrical connections, call a professional.

causes.cause: Hot and neutral wires reversed at the furnace power connection
Hot and neutral reversed at the wall outlet or disconnect switch
Missing or improper ground connection
Faulty control board polarity detection circuit

symptoms: Furnace will not operate
Six LED blinks on the control board
System may have worked after recent electrical work or installation
Flame sensor may not detect flame even when present

fixSteps.title: Turn Off Power
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the furnace at the breaker panel and the power switch on or near the furnace before inspecting any wiring.

fixSteps.title: Check Power Connections
fixSteps.instruction: Open the furnace access panel and locate the incoming power wires. Using a voltage tester, verify that the black (hot) wire is connected to the hot terminal and the white (neutral) wire is on the neutral terminal.

fixSteps.title: Test the Outlet or Disconnect
fixSteps.instruction: Use an outlet tester or multimeter to check the outlet or disconnect switch powering the furnace for correct polarity and proper grounding.

fixSteps.title: Verify Ground Connection
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure the furnace has a solid ground connection. The ground wire should be securely attached to the furnace chassis and connected back to the electrical panel ground bus.

whenToCallPro: If you are not comfortable working with electrical wiring, or if correcting the polarity does not resolve the issue.

faq.q: Why does polarity matter for a furnace?
faq.a: The flame sensing circuit uses the grounding path to detect flame. If polarity is reversed, the flame sensor cannot properly detect the flame, causing ignition failures.

faq.q: Could recent electrical work cause this?
faq.a: Yes, if wiring was recently done on the furnace circuit, an electrician may have inadvertently swapped the hot and neutral wires.

faq.q: How do I check polarity without special tools?
faq.a: You need at minimum an outlet tester (available for under $15 at hardware stores) or a multimeter. There is no safe way to check polarity without a testing device.

title: Gas Valve Circuit Failure
shortDescription: The control board has detected a problem with the gas valve circuit.
fullDescription: Seven LED blinks on a Carrier furnace indicate a gas valve circuit error. The control board has detected an abnormal condition in the gas valve electrical circuit, which could be a shorted or open gas valve coil, wiring issue, or a control board problem.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: Do not attempt to repair or bypass the gas valve. A malfunctioning gas valve is a serious safety concern. If you smell gas, leave immediately and call your gas company.

causes.cause: Faulty gas valve coil (open or shorted)
Loose or damaged wiring between control board and gas valve
Faulty control board gas valve relay
Incorrect gas valve for the furnace model

symptoms: Furnace does not ignite — igniter may glow but no gas flows
Seven LED blinks on the control board
Error code appears during ignition sequence
No clicking sound from gas valve during ignition

fixSteps.title: Power Cycle the Furnace
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off power to the furnace, wait 60 seconds, and restore power. Sometimes a transient condition can trigger this code.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Gas Valve Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: With power off, check the wiring harness between the control board and the gas valve for loose connections, damaged insulation, or corrosion.

fixSteps.title: Test Gas Valve Coil Resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the wires from the gas valve and measure resistance across the valve coil terminals with a multimeter. Compare to the manufacturer specification (typically 10-60 ohms).

fixSteps.title: Contact a Professional
fixSteps.instruction: Gas valve replacement requires a licensed technician. The valve must be leak-tested after installation.

whenToCallPro: Call a professional for any gas valve related issue. Gas valve replacement and testing must be done by a licensed technician.

faq.q: Can I replace the gas valve myself?
faq.a: Gas valve replacement should be performed by a licensed HVAC technician. Improper installation can lead to gas leaks, fire, or carbon monoxide exposure.

faq.q: How much does a gas valve replacement cost?
faq.a: A Carrier gas valve typically costs $100-$300 for the part, with total repair costs of $250-$700 including labor.

faq.q: Could this be just a wiring issue?
faq.a: Yes, sometimes a loose connection is the cause. Checking wiring is a reasonable first step, but if the valve coil tests bad, replacement is needed.

title: Igniter Circuit Failure
shortDescription: The control board detected a problem with the igniter circuit.
fullDescription: Eight LED blinks on a Carrier furnace indicate an igniter circuit failure. The control board has detected that the hot surface igniter circuit is open (broken igniter) or has abnormal resistance. The igniter cannot heat up to light the gas burners.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: Always turn off the furnace and gas supply before handling the igniter. The hot surface igniter is extremely fragile and operates at very high temperatures. Do not touch the igniter element with bare hands as skin oils can cause premature failure.

causes.cause: Cracked or broken hot surface igniter
Loose wiring connection at the igniter
Igniter worn out from age (typical lifespan 3-7 years)
Faulty control board igniter relay

symptoms: Furnace does not ignite — no glow from igniter visible
Eight LED blinks on the control board
Draft inducer runs but ignition never begins
Furnace cycles through startup but no heat is produced

fixSteps.title: Visual Inspection
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the furnace and carefully inspect the hot surface igniter for visible cracks or breaks. The igniter is a small ceramic or silicon nitride element located near the burners.

fixSteps.title: Check Igniter Connections
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure the wire connectors on the igniter are firmly seated. Wiggle the connections gently to check for looseness.

fixSteps.title: Test Igniter Resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the igniter wires and measure resistance with a multimeter. A healthy silicon nitride igniter typically reads 10-200 ohms. An open reading (infinite/OL) confirms a broken igniter.

fixSteps.title: Replace the Igniter
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the single mounting screw, disconnect the old igniter, and install the replacement. Do not touch the new igniter element with bare fingers. Use the exact replacement part number for your model.

whenToCallPro: If you are not comfortable accessing the burner compartment, or if a new igniter does not resolve the issue.

faq.q: How long do furnace igniters last?
faq.a: Hot surface igniters typically last 3-7 years. Silicon nitride igniters tend to last longer than silicon carbide types.

faq.q: Why should I not touch the igniter with my hands?
faq.a: Oils from your skin create hot spots on the igniter element that can cause premature cracking and failure. Always handle by the ceramic base or use gloves.

faq.q: Can I use a universal igniter?
faq.a: Universal igniters can work but may not fit perfectly or last as long. Using the OEM replacement part for your specific Carrier model is recommended.

title: Grounding or Wiring Fault
shortDescription: The control board has detected a wiring or grounding fault in the system.
fullDescription: Nine LED blinks on a Carrier furnace indicate a check for grounding or internal wiring fault. The control board has detected an abnormal condition in the low-voltage or high-voltage wiring that prevents safe operation.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: Turn off the furnace at the breaker and gas supply before inspecting any wiring. Electrical faults can cause shock or fire hazards.

causes.cause: Loose or corroded ground wire connection
Low-voltage wiring shorted to ground
Damaged wire insulation from heat or rodents
Faulty control board

symptoms: Furnace will not operate at all
Nine LED blinks on the control board
System may have failed suddenly without warning
Thermostat may display a blank screen if low-voltage wiring is affected

fixSteps.title: Turn Off Power
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the furnace at the breaker and the local power switch before inspecting any wiring.

fixSteps.title: Inspect All Wiring Connections
fixSteps.instruction: Carefully examine all wire connections on the control board, gas valve, blower motor, and other components for loose, corroded, or damaged connections.

fixSteps.title: Check Ground Connection
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the ground wire is securely attached to the furnace chassis and that the circuit has a proper ground path back to the electrical panel.

fixSteps.title: Inspect for Damaged Insulation
fixSteps.instruction: Look for any wires with damaged or melted insulation, particularly near hot surfaces. Check for rodent damage to wiring. Repair or replace any damaged wires.

whenToCallPro: If you cannot locate the wiring fault, or if the control board appears damaged.

faq.q: Can rodents cause wiring faults?
faq.a: Yes, mice and rats often nest in furnace areas and chew on wire insulation, causing shorts and ground faults.

faq.q: How do I know if the control board is bad?
faq.a: If all wiring checks out and the error persists, the control board may have a fault. Board replacement typically costs $200-$600 installed.

faq.q: Is this an emergency?
faq.a: Not typically an emergency, but the furnace should not be operated until the wiring fault is found and repaired. Use an alternate heat source in the meantime.

title: Normal Operation / No Call for Heat
shortDescription: The LED is on continuously, indicating the control board has power and is operating normally with no active call for heat.
fullDescription: A steady (non-blinking) LED on a Carrier furnace control board indicates normal operation. The board has power and is functioning correctly, but there is no current call for heat from the thermostat. If you are not getting heat, the issue may be with the thermostat, wiring, or settings rather than the furnace itself.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: If you smell gas at any time, leave the home immediately and call your gas company.

causes.cause: Thermostat not calling for heat (normal operation)
Thermostat set incorrectly or batteries dead
Broken thermostat wire between thermostat and furnace
Thermostat set above current room temperature

symptoms: Steady LED on the control board (no blinking)
Furnace not running but no error present
Thermostat display shows normal operation
No heat being produced despite expecting heat

fixSteps.title: Check Thermostat Settings
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the thermostat is set to HEAT mode and the set temperature is above the current room temperature. Also check that the fan is set to AUTO.

fixSteps.title: Replace Thermostat Batteries
fixSteps.instruction: If your thermostat uses batteries, replace them. A low battery can prevent the thermostat from sending a heat signal.

fixSteps.title: Test the Thermostat Signal
fixSteps.instruction: At the furnace control board, briefly connect the R (red) and W (white) thermostat wires together. If the furnace starts, the issue is with the thermostat or wiring, not the furnace.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Thermostat Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check the thermostat wire for damage between the thermostat and furnace. Look for loose connections at both ends.

whenToCallPro: If the thermostat wiring is damaged and needs replacement, or if you cannot determine why the thermostat is not sending a signal.

faq.q: Is a steady LED normal?
faq.a: Yes, a steady LED means the furnace control board has power and is functioning normally with no call for heat. This is the expected state when the thermostat is satisfied.

faq.q: Why is my furnace not running if the LED is steady?
faq.a: A steady LED means the furnace is not receiving a heat signal from the thermostat. Check your thermostat settings, batteries, and wiring.

faq.q: What should the LED look like during normal heating?
faq.a: During a normal heating cycle, the LED will typically blink at a regular pace or display a specific pattern depending on the model. Consult your manual for the specific normal operation pattern.

title: Flame Detected — Normal Heating Operation
shortDescription: Rapid LED blinking indicates the furnace is operating normally and flame is detected.
fullDescription: A rapidly blinking LED on many Carrier furnace models indicates that the furnace is in a normal heating cycle with flame successfully detected. This is not an error code. If you are experiencing a problem despite seeing a rapid blink, the issue may be in the air distribution system rather than the furnace ignition system.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: Even during normal operation, ensure you have working carbon monoxide detectors near the furnace and in sleeping areas.

causes.cause: Normal heating operation (not an error)
Furnace is running but airflow is weak (filter or duct issue)
Blower not distributing heat properly
Thermostat miscalibration causing short cycles

symptoms: Rapidly blinking LED on the control board
Burners are lit and furnace is producing heat
Normal operating sounds from the furnace
Warm air coming from vents

fixSteps.title: Confirm Normal Operation
fixSteps.instruction: A rapid blink indicates the furnace is running normally. Check that warm air is coming from the supply vents in your home.

fixSteps.title: Check Airflow
fixSteps.instruction: If the furnace is running but rooms are not warming, check the air filter and ensure all vents are open and unobstructed.

fixSteps.title: Verify Thermostat Operation
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure the thermostat is reading the correct room temperature. If the furnace short-cycles (runs briefly then stops), the thermostat may need recalibration or relocation.

fixSteps.title: Check for Duct Leaks
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect accessible ductwork for disconnected joints or gaps that may be leaking heated air into unconditioned spaces.

whenToCallPro: If the furnace runs but your home is not heating properly despite clean filters and open vents, a professional can check ductwork and system performance.

faq.q: Is rapid blinking an error?
faq.a: No, on most Carrier furnace models, a rapid blink means the furnace is operating normally with flame detected. It is the normal running indicator.

faq.q: Why is my house still cold if the furnace is running?
faq.a: If the furnace is running normally but your house is cold, check for a dirty filter, closed vents, duct leaks, or an undersized system for your home.

faq.q: How long should a heating cycle last?
faq.a: A typical furnace cycle runs 10-20 minutes. Cycles shorter than 5 minutes (short cycling) or longer than 30 minutes may indicate a problem.

title: No Power to Control Board
shortDescription: The LED is completely off, indicating no power is reaching the furnace control board.
fullDescription: When the diagnostic LED on a Carrier furnace control board is completely dark (not illuminated at all), the board is not receiving power. This could be due to a tripped breaker, blown fuse, disconnected power, or a failed transformer.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: Before working on the furnace, ensure the gas supply is turned off. If you smell gas at any time, leave the home and call your gas company.

causes.cause: Tripped circuit breaker or blown fuse in electrical panel
Furnace power switch turned off
Blown control board fuse
Failed transformer (24V or 120V)
Damaged power wiring to the furnace

symptoms: No LED visible on the control board at all
Furnace completely non-responsive
Thermostat display may be blank (if powered by furnace)
No sounds from the furnace — completely dead

fixSteps.title: Check the Circuit Breaker
fixSteps.instruction: Go to your electrical panel and find the breaker labeled for the furnace. If it is tripped (in the middle position), turn it fully off, then back on.

fixSteps.title: Check the Furnace Power Switch
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the power switch on or near the furnace (often looks like a light switch). Ensure it is in the ON position.

fixSteps.title: Check the Control Board Fuse
fixSteps.instruction: Open the furnace panel and locate the small fuse on the control board (typically 3-5 amp). Check if the fuse element is intact. Replace with the same amperage fuse if blown.

fixSteps.title: Test the Transformer
fixSteps.instruction: Using a multimeter, check the output of the 24V transformer. You should read approximately 24-28 VAC on the secondary side. If there is no output but 120V is present on the primary, the transformer has failed.

whenToCallPro: If the breaker trips repeatedly, or if the transformer or control board has failed.

faq.q: Why does my furnace breaker keep tripping?
faq.a: A repeatedly tripping breaker may indicate a short circuit in the furnace wiring, a failing blower motor drawing too much current, or a problem with the breaker itself. Have it checked by a professional.

faq.q: What size fuse does my Carrier furnace use?
faq.a: Most Carrier furnaces use a 3-amp or 5-amp automotive-style fuse on the control board. Check the markings on the current fuse or the furnace manual for the correct amperage.

faq.q: Why does the control board fuse keep blowing?
faq.a: A repeatedly blown fuse usually indicates a short circuit in the thermostat wiring. Check for damaged thermostat wires or a short at the thermostat itself.

title: Normal Standby — Waiting for Heat Call
shortDescription: A slow, steady blink indicates the furnace is in standby mode, powered on and waiting for a call from the thermostat.
fullDescription: On some Carrier furnace models, a slow blinking LED indicates the system is in normal standby mode. The control board has power, all safety circuits are satisfied, and the furnace is waiting for the thermostat to call for heat. This is not an error code.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: Ensure your furnace area is clear of flammable materials and that you have working carbon monoxide detectors in your home.

causes.cause: Normal standby operation (not an error)
Thermostat not set to call for heat
Room temperature already at or above set point
Thermostat scheduling turned off heat

symptoms: Slow blinking LED on the control board
Furnace is idle but responsive
No heating cycle running
System starts normally when thermostat calls for heat

fixSteps.title: Verify Thermostat Settings
fixSteps.instruction: Check that the thermostat is set to HEAT mode and the set point temperature is above the current room temperature.

fixSteps.title: Check Thermostat Schedule
fixSteps.instruction: If you have a programmable thermostat, check the schedule to ensure it is not in an away or sleep mode that has lowered the set temperature.

fixSteps.title: Raise the Set Point
fixSteps.instruction: Temporarily raise the thermostat 5 degrees above room temperature. The furnace should start within 1-2 minutes if everything is working properly.

fixSteps.title: Listen for Startup
fixSteps.instruction: After raising the thermostat, listen for the furnace to begin its startup sequence: first the inducer motor, then the igniter, then the burners, and finally the blower.

whenToCallPro: Only if raising the thermostat does not cause the furnace to start. The slow blink itself is normal and not a reason to call for service.

faq.q: Is a slow blinking LED normal?
faq.a: Yes, on many Carrier models, a slow blink means the system is in normal standby mode with power and all safety circuits OK.

faq.q: How do I know if my furnace is working?
faq.a: Raise the thermostat above room temperature. You should hear the furnace start within 1-2 minutes. The sequence is: inducer motor, then igniter glow, then burner ignition, then blower starts.

faq.q: Why does my furnace not start even in standby?
faq.a: If the LED shows slow blink (normal standby) but the furnace does not start when thermostat calls for heat, check thermostat wiring, batteries, and settings first.

title: Communicating System Alert
shortDescription: On two-LED models, alternating blinking between the LEDs indicates a communication error between components.
fullDescription: On Carrier furnaces with dual diagnostic LEDs, an alternating blink pattern between the two LEDs indicates a communication fault. This typically applies to Infinity or Performance series communicating systems where the furnace, thermostat, and outdoor unit share a data link. The control board cannot establish or maintain communication with other system components.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: While communication errors are not immediately dangerous, the system may operate in a degraded mode. Ensure CO detectors are functioning.

causes.cause: Thermostat communication wire loose or damaged
Incompatible thermostat or control module
Outdoor unit communication board failure
Control board communication circuit failure

symptoms: Alternating blink pattern between two LEDs
System may operate in backup or degraded mode
Thermostat may show communication error
Some system features may be unavailable

fixSteps.title: Power Cycle All Components
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the furnace, outdoor unit, and thermostat. Wait 2 minutes and restore power to each. Communication often re-establishes after a full system reset.

fixSteps.title: Check Communication Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the communication wire (typically a 4-wire or 2-wire cable) between the furnace and thermostat for damage, loose connections, or disconnected terminals.

fixSteps.title: Verify Thermostat Compatibility
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure the thermostat is a Carrier Infinity-compatible model if you have an Infinity system. Standard thermostats will not communicate properly with communicating systems.

fixSteps.title: Contact Carrier Dealer
fixSteps.instruction: Communicating system issues often require dealer-level diagnostic tools to properly troubleshoot. Contact an authorized Carrier dealer.

whenToCallPro: If power cycling does not restore communication, or if you have a communicating Infinity system that requires dealer tools for diagnosis.

faq.q: What is a communicating HVAC system?
faq.a: A communicating system uses a digital data link between the thermostat, furnace, and outdoor unit to share information and optimize performance. Carrier's Infinity series is a communicating system.

faq.q: Can I use a regular thermostat with a communicating furnace?
faq.a: Some communicating furnaces can operate in non-communicating mode with a standard thermostat, but you lose advanced features. Check your model's compatibility.

faq.q: Will my furnace still heat during a communication error?
faq.a: Many communicating furnaces have a backup mode that provides basic heating even when communication is lost, but at reduced efficiency and without advanced features.

title: Continuous Off/On Cycling
shortDescription: The furnace is rapidly cycling on and off without completing a heating cycle.
fullDescription: Continuous rapid on/off cycling on a Carrier furnace indicates the system is short-cycling. The furnace starts but shuts down before completing a full heating cycle, which can be caused by overheating, thermostat issues, or a control board problem.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: If you smell gas, leave immediately and call your gas company.

causes.cause: Component failure
Wiring issue
Airflow restriction
Age-related wear

symptoms: continuous-off-on LED pattern
System malfunction
No heat or intermittent heat
Unusual sounds or behavior

fixSteps.title: Power Cycle
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off furnace power for 30 seconds, then restore.

fixSteps.title: Check Filter
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the air filter if dirty.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check for loose connections.

fixSteps.title: Call Professional
fixSteps.instruction: If error persists, professional diagnosis is needed.

whenToCallPro: If basic troubleshooting does not resolve the error.

faq.q: What does continuous-off-on mean?
faq.a: The furnace is rapidly cycling on and off without completing a heating cycle.

faq.q: Is this serious?
faq.a: It depends on the specific code. Some are minor and some need immediate attention.

faq.q: How much will repair cost?
faq.a: $100-$500 depending on the issue.
